summary:
The Four Anglo-Mysore Wars (1767–1799 AD) were the most significant military conflicts in the expansion of [[event:2358|British East India Company]] power in South India — a series of battles against the [[event:3273|Kingdom of Mysore]] under Hyder Ali and his son [[event:3275|Tipu Sultan]] that determined whether southern India would remain under indigenous rule or become part of British India. The First War (1767–1769) ended in British defeat: Hyder Ali reached the outskirts of Madras and forced the Company to sign a treaty of mutual assistance. The Second War (1780–1784) began when Hyder Ali attacked the British alliance system; he died during the war, but Tipu Sultan continued it to an inconclusive peace. The Third War (1790–1792) saw a British coalition force half of Tipu's territory, significantly weakening him.

The Fourth and final Anglo-Mysore War (1798–1799) was triggered by Governor-General Wellesley's determination to eliminate Mysore as a French-aligned threat: Tipu Sultan's correspondence with Napoleon and his Revolutionary France made him appear a potential ally in French global ambitions against British India. A massive British-led force of 50,000 men besieged Seringapatam (modern Srirangapatna); Tipu Sultan was killed during the final assault on May 4, 1799, defending the breach in his capital's walls. The British discovery of his negotiations with France and with the Nizam of Hyderabad provided retrospective justification for the war. The victory eliminated the last major indigenous power capable of threatening British southern India and allowed the Company's 'subsidiary alliance' system to extend across the peninsula. The young Arthur Wellesley (later the Duke of Wellington) served in the Mysore campaigns and credited them with his military education.
